claimed_contribution: Generalizes optimal control theory from macro advertising allocation to individual-customer microtargeting.
Develops a multibrand, multimedia microtargeting approach that combines random-effects MNL estimates with an iterative algorithm to compute customer-specific optimal ad exposures.
Demonstrates the value of simultaneous optimization across multiple owned brands using shared single-source customer data.
context: The empirical application is a North American specialty retailer parent company with three apparel-focused brands that are managed separately but share a unified customer database. The setting tracks monthly exposures in email, catalogs, and paid search and corresponding customer purchases.
design_and_data: The paper derives micro-level optimality conditions by combining a Lanchester-style dynamic competition formulation with an individual-level mixed logit choice model. The empirical study uses single-source panel data for 5,000 randomly sampled customers (120,000 customer-month observations) over 24 months (July 2010 to June 2012), including purchases and ad touchpoints for three brands across email, catalog, and paid search. A hierarchical Bayes random-effects MNL is estimated via MCMC in SAS, with controls for demographics, price, organic search, website visits, prior online and in-store sales, seasonality, and endogeneity control functions. The estimated parameters are used to compare current scheduling, macro optimization, separate-brand microtargeting, simultaneous multibrand microtargeting, and a fixed-budget variant, and the paper also runs a simulation varying ad-response heterogeneity.
main_findings: In the retail application, separate-brand optimal microtargeting increases parent-company profit by 150% versus the current ad schedule, while simultaneous multibrand microtargeting increases profit by 183%.
Macro optimal scheduling raises profit by 14%, much less than microtargeting, indicating large gains from customer-level tailoring of exposures.

In simulation, expected profit from microtargeting increases as ad-response heterogeneity increases, because spending can be shifted toward highly responsive customers and away from unresponsive customers.
one_line_summary: The paper develops an optimal-control microtargeting framework that uses individual-level mixed-logit advertising responses to set customer-specific exposures across brands and media, and shows large profit gains in a multibrand retail application.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior optimal-control advertising models that focused on aggregate macro allocation (including multibrand settings) to individual-level multibrand microtargeting with multiple media.
research_gap: Prior optimal-control advertising work had focused on aggregate macro budgeting and media allocation and had not been applied to individual-customer microtargeting. The paper also argues that common rule-based targeting is often nonoptimal because it can target likely buyers and does not jointly incorporate individual ad response, media cost, category spend, and brand share.
research_question: How can optimal control theory be adapted from macro advertising allocation to determine optimal ad exposures at the individual-customer level across multiple media and brands? The paper also asks whether simultaneous multibrand microtargeting with single-source data outperforms macro scheduling and separate brand-by-brand optimization.
